Adventure 5. Lights. Camera. Change the action. A few days later, when I arrived at the bookstore, I found Tom already there, sipping coffee in the cafe and jotting down notes on a small piece of paper. So, said Tom the moment I sat down. Did you find any magazines that fit the bill? Yes, I said. I landed on a magazine about horses. And what did you learn from it? Well, let me just say that I have zero interest in horses. So at first, I couldn't see what I could possibly apply from all the articles about curing horse ailments, buying horses, stabling horses, and breeding horses. But then, I saw an advertisement for a horse farm that really stood out, because it listed all the reasons why you should board your horse with them. They went into great detail about how they take care of the horses, the type of accommodations, and the type and quality of bedding they use. It was so captivating that I found myself wishing I had my own horse just so I could have them look after it. And that made me think that at our bookstore, we never tell people why they should shop with us, rather than at one of the big chains. Or at one of the other independent bookstores in town. So I made a list of the top 10 reasons why someone should come here to buy their books. From the personal, expert service you get from clerks who really know and love books, to free refills on coffee, and we're going to post it in the store and on our website. And use it in one way or another in all our advertising. I passed Tom a copy of the list. Very impressive, he commented, scanning it over. You've really extracted all the reasons that make your store a unique place for people to buy books. Shows real horse sense. I'll overlook that horsing around, I said. In exchange for a few more of those boxes, I was just thinking, said Tom, looking around the store and seeming to ignore my request. What an interesting thing a business is. It's so much like a machine, with all the parts and processes and interactions, stocking, ordering, staffing, advertising, and selling. And while I was waiting for you, I've been sitting here watching the gears turn. People come in, look for books, find them or don't, pay the cashier, and head out the door. Actually, sometimes they don't pay the cashier, I interrupted. They take us up on our new offer to keep the book for a few days before we charge their card. And how's that been working out? Tom leaned forward in his seat, showing genuine interest. Great! Nobody's tried to cheat us. In fact, everyone's gone out of their way to be fair. I can definitely see where it has increased sales and profits, just like encouraging people to call us before they come in and tell us what books they're looking for. I could immediately see a boost in our average sale per customer. Glad to hear it, said Tom. Anyway, as I sat here watching all the actions that make a bookstore or a bookstore, I thought this would be a good setting to look at boxes that have to do with changing the action. In these boxes, instead of looking at the parts and how we might change them, we look at the action itself. For example, I call the first action box, Switch Gears. In this box, you change the speed or quantity of the action. You speed it up, slow it down, do more. Or my personal favorite, do less. Speeding up is interesting because many things that won't work at one speed will work at a different speed. Like water skiing. Go slow and you sink. But go faster, and something almost magical happens. Water builds up under the skis and the pressure keeps you elevated. It's the same physics that keeps an airplane in the sky, of course. And remember how many great ideas you came up with when I pushed you to rapidly write as many as you could in a set amount of time? I nodded with pride. I sure do. The speed definitely pushed me beyond what I thought possible. Should we try it again? Proposed, Tom. Why don't we see how many ideas you can come up with by switching gears over the course of five minutes. This time, I'll scribble down the ideas on this notepad as you come up with them. Let's see, I began, trying to get the creative equivalent of a running start. Tom set the timer on his phone. What could I speed up? I tapped my fingers on the coffee table in front of me, while I looked up and noticed a line forming around the cashier counter. I know, I could speed up the time it takes people to pay for their purchases. People hate to wait in line. That would give me another advantage I could promote over the chains. I could also speed up the amount of time it takes for people to receive books that they special order with express delivery. When people want a book, they don't want to wait, so the faster the better. I mean, I'll struggle to compete with the likes of Amazon on speed, but I can certainly expedite the process. Good. And what could you slow down? Tom prodded. Well, I recently read a study that showed that the longer people spend in a bookstore, the more they buy. I could slow them down with more chairs and tables and install fiber optic Wi-Fi that works at lightning speed from anywhere in the store. Now, how can I do more? I glance at the timer. I still had two minutes. More ways to stock more books. More ways to find out about books. Maybe a website or app where they can look up books themselves. And more books in the store would mean more sales. I could do that by adding more bookcases and by using some of the back rooms for special displays. Less. Push Tom. Who'd been busily jotting down my ideas on his notepad. Let's see. What if I did less? I'm the best salesperson I got, but lately I spend all my time ordering, hiring, and solving problems. What if I let other people handle all that and I went around helping customers? I know sales would improve. You know what? I could do it. Tom interrupted my brainstorming just as the timer on his phone started dinging. He used his pen to count the points he'd written down. Well, that seems like about a dozen. Care to guess what the next action box would be? Let's see, I replied. We've slowed it down, sped it up, done more, and done less. What else can you do to an action? You could change the periodicity he offered. The what? Sorry. What I mean is, you can adjust an action by changing it from something that happens all the time to something that only happens at certain intervals, or vice versa. Change it from something that happens every once in a while to something that happens continuously. I call it the revise the rhythm box. For example, I recently did some work for a mining company. They kept trying to speed up the drilling by messing with the drill bit, changing the shape, changing the material it was made of, making it rotate faster, and so on. But I found that, due to the nature of the material they were drilling through, the best way to speed up the drilling was to change the operation from continuous to intermittent. That is, to drill in pulses rather than continuously. Same principle works above ground. Often doing something in short bursts is more effective than plugging away at it continuously, and vice versa. Kind of like my lawn sprinkler, I suggested. Instead of constantly flooding my lawn with water, it pulses the water intermittently, and suddenly I had an idea. You know, this could be a solution to my messy storeroom problem. I've been looking at it as a continuous action. It should be kept clean and neat all the time. But what if I stopped trying to make it continuously neat and tidy? What if instead I looked at it as an intermittent action, and twice a day, say at 11 and 4, we can make it someone's responsibility to go in and straighten it up? Good, said Tom. Now, how about an idea that goes from intermittent to continuous? I looked at him inquisitively. Take just-in-time delivery, for example, Tom went on. Instead of taking deliveries periodically and needing huge warehouses, factories take delivery continuously as they use up their stock. That would really solve a big chunk of my storeroom problems. But I doubt I could ever get my publishers to agree to a more staggered delivery schedule. Wait, Tom interrupted. Don't be so quick to dismiss an idea as inapplicable just because it seems challenging. Maybe you can't get your publishers to agree to something like just-in-time delivery. But perhaps they would agree to, or you could arrange, something that has some of the elements of just-in-time delivery, such as a speedier delivery service, more deliveries more often, and so on. Even if you can't change a thing about how they deliver, perhaps there are ways you could make your own operation more just-in-time. How can you better anticipate when you're about to run out of a book? How can you get the order to the publisher more quickly, and so on and so on? I bet there's even some affordable AI tech out there for this. Anyways, that's something to look into. Again, it's all how you ask the question. If the question is, can I get all my publishers to do just-in-time delivery like suppliers do for some factories, then the answer is probably not. And idea-making stops. But let's say that instead you ask, what about the idea of just-in-time delivery can improve how we order, stock, and get books delivered? Then your mind opens to possibility and opportunity. Well, I suppose we are a bit behind the times in many of those areas, I admitted. Much as I would like to, I can't blame everything on the publishers. Do you think I could take another whack at taking something from the intermittent to continuous box? Whack away, Tom smiled and nodded. One of the biggest needs is more training for my team. The more they know about books, the more they can help customers find what they're looking for, or even help them find books that they aren't looking for but would really enjoy. I know the online sellers have fancy recommendation engines that do this, but we can offer it from a human perspective, which I think people would appreciate as a unique touch. But our training has always been so intermittent, a day or two when they start work and an occasional seminar after they've been with us long enough. What if we made training continuous? Like getting together right after closing every day to discuss the latest books. Or I could write a newsletter for my employees. They could take it home, read the articles, and get excited about all the new releases, which they could then recommend. That's something I would really enjoy doing that would be really useful. At that moment, we watched a young woman who had been nursing a cup of coffee at a nearby table get up to leave the store. It wasn't the first time I'd noticed her. She came in frequently, usually on Wednesdays, generally a slow day for us, and she would occasionally ask my advice on new releases. We seemed to like a lot of the same books, but she never got more than a coffee at the cafe, which wasn't, I thought to myself, exactly going to put my daughter through college. Or even buy her a textbook. Just a cup of coffee, said Tom, shaking his head as if reading my thoughts. Not much profit in that, I'd imagine. No, unfortunately, not nearly as much as on the pastries. I've been watching her, Tom went on, scrunching his eyes. I could see she wanted a pastry. I noticed her glancing at the display case every once in a while, but she didn't seem to want to stand in line. Nor did she dare to ask the salesperson for one. Maybe it's a dietary thing, who knows. In any case, she never got up to get one. Now, am I right to imagine that if more people also bought a pastry, that would help to fatten up your bottom line? It sure would. Good. Because I took the liberty of rummaging around in a couple of boxes for ideas to see if we couldn't somehow satisfy her sweet tooth. And I came across one in the final action box. D. I. I. D. I. Why? I asked. Do it yourself? No. D. I. I. Do it itself. Let the part or system do it itself. Let it fix itself, or let it perform some other additional and useful function. For example, there used to be a problem with throwing rice at weddings. All that rice had to be picked up afterward, and it was a pain in the neck. But then someone asked, how could this rice clean itself up? And the answer was bird seed. By using bird seed instead of rice, birds would flock down after the celebration and clean up the mess. Or like the clever fellow who, instead of trying to make yet another oven cleaner, decided to let the oven D. I. I. The self-cleaning oven? Exactly. And instead of a stronger tire? A self-sealing one. And instead of a better way for you to blow up an inflatable mattress? A self-inflating one. Now, said Tom, back to that woman who left without buying a pastry. Let her do it herself? Let her serve herself? But how? Tom tilted his head, but said nothing, allowing me to work this out on my own. I suppose that instead of having the pastries behind a glass case, we could put them out in the open where people could just come and serve themselves. I bet if it were that easy to get a pastry, many more people would buy one with their coffee. The payment part would be easy to figure out. We could put a slip of paper under each pastry plate that customers could take to a cashier at their convenience. Or they could pay for it when they pay for the books. You may be on to something. Test your hypothesis. Try it out. Do an experiment. I will. By the way, added Tom somewhat reluctantly. Do you know her? Who? That woman? Not really. Well, yes, sort of. Why do you ask? I don't mean to be nosy. We inventors are just naturally inquisitive. I was just wondering because of your reaction when I pointed her out. You don't miss much, do you? I smiled and chuckled. Her name is Donna. We've had a few brief conversations, but that's it. I'd like to get to know her better, but I haven't dated much since my wife died, and I guess I feel a bit awkward about approaching a customer like that. So, you're the owner, and that's a negative, right? Tom asks slowly. Yes, you could say that. Hey, I see what you're getting at. The make it a benefit box, right? Tom nodded, a huge grin taking over his face. That's right. Turn any negative into a positive. Well, what if I just go up to her and be honest and just tell her my dilemma? It would certainly be a way to start a conversation anyway. Might work, agreed Tom, especially if you fire that pastry.
